rationale: Title Y is stronger overall for JACC because it is concise, readable, and uses a clean registry-outcomes structure. It preserves the core intervention, lead issue, and registry source without overloading the title. Title X is more informative, especially by naming clinical and lead-related outcomes, CIED leads, and multinational scope, but it is long and less elegant for a JACC-style title.
loser_weakness: Title X is weakened by excessive length and stacked qualifiers, making it feel more like an abstract heading than a sharp JACC manuscript title.
